We use, and expect to increasingly use, emerging technologies, including AI, across our operations, including business processes, services and products

Expanded use of AI, including emerging third party AI services and autonomous AI agents, may result in increased data risk, unpredictable system interactions, inadequate controls or safeguards, AI failure, or produce unintended operations or consequences.

The interconnectedness and complexity of AI models may complicate oversight and compliance, and reliance on third party AI models further increases our risks due to limited visibility into their training data, methodologies and safeguards.

AI lowers the entry barriers to plan and execute cyberattacks, enables more personalized and harder to detect social engineering, and improves vulnerability discovery, which may result in the increased likelihood of exploitation

Excerpts are heavily weighted toward risk-factor and operational-risk sections; business/strategy sections describing AI product deployments or revenue contributions may exist in the full filing but are not present here.

Governance frameworks for AI risk are named (Global Compliance, Operational Risk teams, Enterprise Model Risk Policy) but no quantified risk metrics or named oversight body with formal charter details are provided, capping risk disclosure at 4 rather than 5.
